front: In a metric space (X, d), what is the formal definition of a limit point (or accumulation point) of a subset A ⊆ X?
back: A point x ∈ X is a limit point of A if every open ball centered at x contains a point of A different from x itself. Formally: ∀ ε > 0, B(x, ε) ∩ (A \ {x}) ≠ ∅.

front: What is the definition of an isolated point of a subset A in a metric space?
back: A point a ∈ A is an isolated point of A if there exists an ε > 0 such that the open ball B(a, ε) contains no other points of A. That is, B(a, ε) ∩ A = {a}.

front: How are the set of all limit points of A (denoted A') and the closure of A (denoted cl(A)) related?
back: The closure of a set A is equal to the union of A and its set of limit points: cl(A) = A ∪ A'.

front: True or False: In a metric space, a finite set can have limit points.
back: False. A finite set has no limit points. Every point in a finite set is an isolated point because you can choose ε small enough to separate each point from the others.

front: What is the key characteristic that distinguishes a limit point from an isolated point within a set A?
back: A limit point of A can be approximated arbitrarily closely by other points in A (it may or may not be in A itself). An isolated point is a member of A that has a neighborhood where it is the only point from A.
